coredata.dc:title: A one-step ultrasonic irradiation assisted strategy for the preparation of polymer-functionalized carbon quantum dots and their biological imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Hongye
Cui, Yi
Liu, Meiying
Chen, Junyu
Wan, Qing
Wen, Yuanqing
Deng, Fengjie
Zhou, Naigen
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNs) have gradually become the most promising alternative candidates to other traditional fluorescent nanomaterials for biological applications on account of their excellent fluorescence property and remarkable biocompatibility. Although many methods have reported on the preparation of FCNs, to date, no studies have reported the preparation of polymers of functionalized FCNs. A high-efficiency method was developed in this work to synthesize high-quality poly(ethylene oxide) (PEG)-functionalized FCNs from cigarette ash and thiol group-containing PEG via a facile one-pot ultrasonic irradiation treatment. A series of characterization techniques demonstrated the uniform nanoscale size, good fluorescence stability, high water dispersibility and remarkable biocompatibility of the generated FCNs. Furthermore, cell imaging was easily achieved at high resolution using the synthetic FCNs as probes, which validates their potential for bioimaging applications. In summary, an efficient one-pot strategy is reported for the first time on the preparation of PEG-functionalized FCNs with the assistance of ultrasonic irradiation. This method should be of great research interest for the fabrication of other polymer-functionalized FCNs with designable properties and functions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasonic irradiation treatment
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles
Polymer functionalized FCNs
Biological imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016 , 21474057 , 21564006 , 21561022 , 21644014 , 21788102 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072 , 20161BAB213066 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized FT-IR spectra of cigarette ash and FCNs.
1H NMR spectrum of the generated FCNs. Many characterization peaks located between 3 and 4 ppm are observed in the 1H NMR spectrum of FCNs.
TEM images of FCNs (A) and enlarged TEM image of FCNs (B).
DLS FCN data in aqueous solution and the PDI is 0.320.
XPS spectra of FCNs. (A) C 1s region, (B) O 1s region, (C) N 1s region, (D) S 2p region.

(A) Emission PL spectra of FCNs in aqueous solution with different excitation wavelengths. (B) Optimal excitation and emission spectra of FCNs, the excitation wavelength was set at 380 nm. Inset: Photograph taken with sunlight (left cuvette) and with UV lamp (365 nm) (right cuvette).
(A) FL spectra of FCNs in water at different pH values. (B) Fluorescence intensity value versus time under irradiation by a UV lamp (365 nm) for 12 h.
Cell viability of PEG-functionalized FCNs based on the CCK-8 assay. L929 cells were incubated with different concentrations of FCNs up to 160 µg mL−1 for 8 and 24 h.
Cell imaging of FCNs. (A) Fluorescent images, (B) bright field images, and (C) an overlap image of A and B, scale bar = 20 µm. Cells were excited by a laser t a wavelength of 405 nm.
Schematic showing the preparation of PEG-functionalized FCNs. The detailed procedure for the synthesis of PEG-SH is listed in the supporting information.
Element content (%) of PEG, cigarette ash and FCNs was calculated based on XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: A one-step ultrasonic irradiation assisted strategy for the preparation of polymer-functionalized carbon quantum dots and their biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanotechnology and Institute of Biomedical Technology, Chung-Yuan Christian University, Chung-Li 32023, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanotechnology and Institute of Biomedical Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China (Y. Wei).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNs) have gradually become the most promising alternative candidates to other traditional fluorescent nanomaterials for biological applications on account of their excellent fluorescence property and remarkable biocompatibility. Although many methods have reported on the preparation of FCNs, to date, no studies have reported the preparation of polymers of functionalized FCNs. A high-efficiency method was developed in this work to synthesize high-quality poly(ethylene oxide) (PEG)-functionalized FCNs from cigarette ash and thiol group-containing PEG via a facile one-pot ultrasonic irradiation treatment. A series of characterization techniques demonstrated the uniform nanoscale size, good fluorescence stability, high water dispersibility and remarkable biocompatibility of the generated FCNs. Furthermore, cell imaging was easily achieved at high resolution using the synthetic FCNs as probes, which validates their potential for bioimaging applications. In summary, an efficient one-pot strategy is reported for the first time on the preparation of PEG-functionalized FCNs with the assistance of ultrasonic irradiation. This method should be of great research interest for the fabrication of other polymer-functionalized FCNs with designable properties and functions.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, great progress and advances have been made in the fabrication and application of functional nanomaterials, especially fluorescent nanomaterials [1–3]. Various fluorescent nanomaterials with different compositions and fluorescence properties have been reported in succession over the past few decades, such as semiconductor quantum dots [4], photoluminescent silicon nanoparticles [5–7], metallic nanoclusters [8,9] and fluorescent organic nanoparticles [10–13]. Nevertheless, these traditional fluorescent nanomaterials have native disadvantages, which limit their further practical applications. For instance, the most fluorescent organic dyes suffer from fluorescence quenching at the aggregation state, which is known as the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect [14]. Although the fluorescent organic nanoparticles with aggregtion-induced emission (AIE) feature could overcome the ACQ effect of the fluorescent materials, the procedures for synthesis of AIE-active organic molecules and fabrication of AIE-active fluorescent organic nanoparticles are rather complex and expensive [15–20]. Furthermore, semiconductor quantum dots encounter the problem of high cytotoxicity owing to their heavy metal compositions [21]. Therefore, the synthesis of novel fluorescence nanomaterials with excellent biological and optical properties is urgently required. Fortunately, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNs) are regarded as one of the most promising alternative candidates to traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots because of their facile synthetic routes, high chemical stability and low toxicity [22,23]. In light of these advantages, FCNs are currently among the most active research topics in various applications, such as optoelectronic devices, biological labeling, biosensors and bioimaging [24].

The outstanding characteristics of FCNs are primarily determined by the carbon source [25]. Therefore, the selection of the carbon source is vital for the synthesis of FCNs. Recently, a large amount of research has focused on using natural sources as carbon precursors due to the abundance of carbon precursors and their excellent biocompatibility [26,27]. There are already numerous reports on the preparation of FCNs from natural sources, including milk [28], cabbage [29], lemon juice [30] and orange juice [31]. Cigarette smoking continues to be a major health hazard and gives rise to significant cardiovascular morbidity and mortality [32]. However, cigarette ash is very useful in our daily lives, as it can be used to relieve itching, to provide deodorization and for degreasing. According to previous research, cigarette ash is a carbon-rich material that is suitable for use as a carbon source [33]. More importantly, cigarette ash is an inexpensive and renewable biomaterial. Nevertheless, there are no reports regarding the use of cigarette ash as a carbon source.

Various preparation methods of FCNs have been developed to date, such as hydrothermal, laser ablation, electrochemical synthesis and microwave irradiation [34–37]. The hydrothermal method is the most common preparation method of FCNs. However, the above mentioned procedures, including the hydrothermal method, require several steps, harsh chemical reactions and highs costs. It is therefore desirable to fabricate FCNs using facile and commercially available methods. Ultrasonic synthesis has emerged as the most promising alternative candidate to traditional preparation methods [38,39]. It has been widely demonstrated that multitudinous reactions can be accelerated via shorter reaction times and can provide higher yield under ultrasonication [39]. In particular, miniprobe sonography is able to generate tremendous local energy in the reaction, which promotes carbonization for the formation of FCNs. Nevertheless, only limited studies have described a high-efficiency preparation method of FCNs through ultrasonic synthesis [40–42]. Moreover, the preparation of polymer-functionalized FCNs through ultrasonic irradiation treatment has not been reported thus far.

In this contribution, we developed a facile one-pot strategy to synthesize high-quality polymer-functionalized FCNs via an ultrasonic irradiation treatment using cigarette ash as a cheap and renewable carbon source and further explore their potential biological applications. The detailed experimental procedures are clearly described in Scheme 1 . Poly(ethylene oxide) (PEG) functionalized FCNs were prepared through an ultrasonic irradiation treatment of cigarette ash and PEG-SH, which was synthesized via a dehydration reaction (Fig. S1). The introduction of PEG not only improved the water dispersibility of FCNs but also endowed the FCNs with biocompatibility. Compared with a traditional preparation method, this strategy using a one-step ultrasonic treatment requires no harsh synthetic conditions or complex experimental procedures. More importantly, the resultant FCNs show excellent performance in cytotoxicity assessment and cell imaging.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.07.099.

All chemical agents were of analytical grade and were used as received without any further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with doubly distilled water. All chemicals, such as methoxypolyethylene glycol (PEG, Mw: 1900 Da), mercaptoacetic acid (Mw: 92.12 Da, 98%), dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC, Mw: 206.33 Da, 98%) and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP, Mw: 122.17 Da, 99%), were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Cigarettes were purchased from supermarkets located in Nanchang.
The Ultrasonic Cell Disruptor (YMNL-500) was purchased from Nanjing Immanuel Instruments Co. LTD. The detailed characterization information of samples was listed in the supporting information.
The Ultrasonic Cell Disruptor (YMNL-500) was purchased from Nanjing Immanuel Instruments Co. LTD. The detailed characterization information of samples was listed in the supporting information.

PEG-SH were synthesized via a dehydration reaction, similar to a previous report [43]. Briefly, PEG (4 g), mercaptoacetic acid (200 mg), DCC (500 mg) and DMAP (50 mg) were dissolved in 25 mL of anhydrous toluene. The reaction was stirred at 45 °C for 24 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Diethyl ether (200 mL) was added to the mixture after the end of the reaction. The white powder separated out of the solution. Finally, we obtained pure PEG-SH via centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 8 min followed by an ethanol wash until the filtrate was clear.

Cigarette ash was collected by placing a glass plate on top of a lit cigarette. Cigarette ash (1 g) and PEG-SH (700 mg) were dissolved in 30 mL of DMF. The mixture was ultrasonically heated for 30 min using an ultrasonic cell disruptor. Supernatant liquor was obtained via centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 8 min. Finally, the supernatant liquor was dialyzed against distilled water and alcohol for 3 days to remove any remaining raw materials. We obtained the final FCNs via rotary evaporation.

The assessment of cell viability aimed to evaluate the possible biomedical applications of the FCN composites by a cell-counting kits 8 (CCK-8) assay using L929 cells [44–47]. The detailed informtion for the evaluation of cell viability was listed in the supporting information.

The assessment of cell viability aimed to evaluate the possible biomedical applications of the FCN composites by a cell-counting kits 8 (CCK-8) assay using L929 cells [44–47]. The detailed informtion for the evaluation of cell viability was listed in the supporting information.
The cell uptake behavior of FCNs towards L929 cells was examined using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zeiss 710 3-channel (Zeiss, Germany) at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

The FT-IR spectra and 1H NMR spectra were examined to confirm the successful synthesis of FCNs. As shown in Fig. 1 , it is obvious that a series of characteristic peaks appeared in the FT-IR spectra of cigarette ash and FCNs. Obvious peaks at 3400, 1100 and 1450 cm−1 were observed in the spectrum of pristine cigarette ash, which were attributed to the OH, O and Shear deformation vibrations of methylene. Due to carbonization via ultrasonication, the Shear deformation vibration of methylene at 1450 cm−1 disappeared in the FCNs. Compared with pristine cigarette ash, two new peaks at 2900 and 1100 cm−1 appeared in the spectra of FCNs, corresponding to CH2 and CO from PEG. Moreover, the peak intensities at 3400 and 1734 cm−1 were significantly enhanced compared with the FT-IR spectrum of cigarette ash. The above changes clearly indicate that many functional groups, such as OH and CO, existed in FCNs. The presence of OH and CO implied that PEG was integrated into FCNs. Therefore, we conclude that PEG-functionalized FCNs were fabricated via the ultrasonic irradiation treatment.

The 1H NMR spectra of PEG-SH and FCNs are displayed in Fig. S2 and Fig. 2 , respectively. The solvent is D2O, and the solvent peak is approximately 4.8 ppm. A series of peaks with chemical shifts at 3–4 ppm are observed, which suggests the successful preparation of PEG-SH. In addition, the small peaks should be impurity peak from the DMAP and DCC. The 1H NMR spectrum of FCNs also shows similar chemical shifts between 3 and 4 ppm, suggesting that PEG-SH was introduced into FCNs. Furthermore, it is difficult to dissolve cigarette ash completely in water and organic solvents, which led to no clear 1H NMR (Fig. S3) and 13C NMR (Fig. S4) spectra for cigarette ash. Fig. S5 shows the 13C NMR spectra of FCNs. Therefore, 1H NMR spectra convincingly demonstrate the successful fabrication of PEG-functionalized FCNs via ultrasonic synthesis. Moreover, the Raman spectrum of FCNs is shown in Fig. S6. The FCNs possess the D band at 1326 cm−1 and the G band at 1626 cm−1 [48,49]. The results from Raman spectrum indicated that both graphitic domains and defect were existed in FCNs. Although many strategies have previously been reported for the preparation of FCNs, to date, no reports have demonstrated a direct preparation of polymer-functionalized FCNs through a one-step method. More importantly, we were also able to introduce other functional components on these FCNs using different thiol-group-containing molecules.

The 1H NMR spectra of PEG-SH and FCNs are displayed in Fig. S2 and Fig. 2 , respectively. The solvent is D2O, and the solvent peak is approximately 4.8 ppm. A series of peaks with chemical shifts at 3–4 ppm are observed, which suggests the successful preparation of PEG-SH. In addition, the small peaks should be impurity peak from the DMAP and DCC. The 1H NMR spectrum of FCNs also shows similar chemical shifts between 3 and 4 ppm, suggesting that PEG-SH was introduced into FCNs. Furthermore, it is difficult to dissolve cigarette ash completely in water and organic solvents, which led to no clear 1H NMR (Fig. S3) and 13C NMR (Fig. S4) spectra for cigarette ash. Fig. S5 shows the 13C NMR spectra of FCNs. Therefore, 1H NMR spectra convincingly demonstrate the successful fabrication of PEG-functionalized FCNs via ultrasonic synthesis. Moreover, the Raman spectrum of FCNs is shown in Fig. S6. The FCNs possess the D band at 1326 cm−1 and the G band at 1626 cm−1 [48,49]. The results from Raman spectrum indicated that both graphitic domains and defect were existed in FCNs. Although many strategies have previously been reported for the preparation of FCNs, to date, no reports have demonstrated a direct preparation of polymer-functionalized FCNs through a one-step method. More importantly, we were also able to introduce other functional components on these FCNs using different thiol-group-containing molecules.

The XPS provides detailed information regarding the chemical compositions and functional groups in FCNs. It is noteworthy that four obvious peaks with binding energies at 164, 285, 399 and 533 eV are found in the FCNs (Fig. S7), which are assigned to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) are shown in Fig. 5 A-D, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5A) can be divided into three peaks located at 284.9, 286.3 and 288.9 eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, COC and OCO, respectively. In addition, the different chemical compositions of the FCNs was calculated based on the XPS spectra. As observed in Table 1 , the atomic ratios of C, O, N and S in FCNs were 73.08%, 23.51%, 2.70% and 0.71%, respectively. The S should originate from PEG-SH, indicating the successful preparation of PEG-functionalized FCNs. As compared with the FCNs, the XPS spectra of PEG-SH and cigarette ash were also examined. The detailed results were displayed in the supporting information (Figs. S8–S11). It can be seen that four elements such as C, O, N, S were detected in the XPS spectra of PEG-SH and cigarette ash. This indicated that the thiol group has been introduced on PEG-SH. Moreover, it can be seen that the percentage of N in the final FCNs is obviously increased as compared with that of PEG-SH and cigarette ash (Table 1). This implied that the solvent DMF may involve in the formation of FCNs. On the other hand, the content of C was also signficantly increased in the sample of FCNs as compared with that of PEG-SH and cigarette ash (Table 1). It is likely ascribed to loss of oxygen during the formation of conjugated systems.

The XPS provides detailed information regarding the chemical compositions and functional groups in FCNs. It is noteworthy that four obvious peaks with binding energies at 164, 285, 399 and 533 eV are found in the FCNs (Fig. S7), which are assigned to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) are shown in Fig. 5 A-D, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5A) can be divided into three peaks located at 284.9, 286.3 and 288.9 eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, COC and OCO, respectively. In addition, the different chemical compositions of the FCNs was calculated based on the XPS spectra. As observed in Table 1 , the atomic ratios of C, O, N and S in FCNs were 73.08%, 23.51%, 2.70% and 0.71%, respectively. The S should originate from PEG-SH, indicating the successful preparation of PEG-functionalized FCNs. As compared with the FCNs, the XPS spectra of PEG-SH and cigarette ash were also examined. The detailed results were displayed in the supporting information (Fig. S8–S11). It can be seen that four elements such as C, O, N, S were detected in the XPS spectra of PEG-SH and cigarette ash. This indicated that the thiol group has been introduced on PEG-SH. Moreover, it can be seen that the percentage of N in the final FCNs is obviously increased as compared with that of PEG-SH and cigarette ash (Table 1). This implied that the solvent DMF may involve in the formation of FCNs. On the other hand, the content of C was also signficantly increased in the sample of FCNs as compared with that of PEG-SH and cigarette ash (Table 1). It is likely ascribed to loss of oxygen during the formation of conjugated systems.

Bases on all of aforementioned characterizations, it is shown that FCNs were successfully prepared using combination of PEG polymer and cigarette ash under ultrasonication. The PEG polymer improves the water dispersibility of FCNs. The cigarette ash serves as the carbon source to form carbon dots, which emit bright green fluorescence. Therefore, we further evaluated the optical properties of FCNs. First, we found that the emission wavelengths of FCNs were dependent on the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 6 A) possibly due to multiexcitation centers that exist in these FCNs. This emission phenomenon was also found in many other FCNs from other preparation methods. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity also changed when these FCNs were excited by various excitation wavelengths. The emission wavelength with maximum intensity was obtained when these FCNs were excited with a 380 nm excitation wavelength Fig. 6B. We also performed ultrasonication of PEG-SH for comparison. The excitation wavelength was 380 nm, and the emission wavelength spectrum of ultrasonicated PEG-SH is shown in Fig. S12, which possesses a weak peak at 450 nm. In addition, the left cuvette of the insert in Fig. 6B is the aqueous solution of FCNs under the sunlight. The aqueous solution of FCNs is transparent, and the marker “FCNs” as background could be distinctly observed, indicating the excellent water dispersibility of FCNs. Furthermore, an aqueous solution of FCNs emits bright green fluorescence under a 365-nm UV lamp irradiation (right cuvette of inset in Fig. 6B). Furthermore, the UV–Vis spectrum of FCNs is show in Fig. S13. It is obvious that an adsorption peak at 233 nm is observed in the spectrum, which is attributed to the π → π* transition of CC and aromatic rings, suggesting the successful formation of the conjugate structure after ultrasonic irradiation. In previous reports, the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots using PEG as the precusor was also reported [41,50]. It has been demonstrated that the fluorescent carbon dots and and carbon dots coated Sn nanocompositew with blue emission could be obtained through direct ultrasonic treatment. As compared with the previous reports, the FCNs obtained from this work using the cigarette ash and PEG-SH emits green-yellow color. It indicates that the cigarette ash with aromatic rings has been modified with PEG-SH. The relative large conjugation systems will result in long emission wavelength under similar excitation wavelength.

Bases on all of aforementioned characterizations, it is shown that FCNs were successfully prepared using combination of PEG polymer and cigarette ash under ultrasonication. The PEG polymer improves the water dispersibility of FCNs. The cigarette ash serves as the carbon source to form carbon dots, which emit bright green fluorescence. Therefore, we further evaluated the optical properties of FCNs. First, we found that the emission wavelengths of FCNs were dependent on the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 6 A) possibly due to multiexcitation centers that exist in these FCNs. This emission phenomenon was also found in many other FCNs from other preparation methods. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity also changed when these FCNs were excited by various excitation wavelengths. The emission wavelength with maximum intensity was obtained when these FCNs were excited with a 380 nm excitation wavelength Fig. 6B. We also performed ultrasonication of PEG-SH for comparison. The excitation wavelength was 380 nm, and the emission wavelength spectrum of ultrasonicated PEG-SH is shown in Fig. S12, which possesses a weak peak at 450 nm. In addition, the left cuvette of the insert in Fig. 6B is the aqueous solution of FCNs under the sunlight. The aqueous solution of FCNs is transparent, and the marker “FCNs” as background could be distinctly observed, indicating the excellent water dispersibility of FCNs. Furthermore, an aqueous solution of FCNs emits bright green fluorescence under a 365-nm UV lamp irradiation (right cuvette of inset in Fig. 6B). Furthermore, the UV–Vis spectrum of FCNs is show in Fig. S13. It is obvious that an adsorption peak at 233 nm is observed in the spectrum, which is attributed to the π → π* transition of CC and aromatic rings, suggesting the successful formation of the conjugate structure after ultrasonic irradiation. In previous reports, the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots using PEG as the precusor was also reported [41,50]. It has been demonstrated that the fluorescent carbon dots and and carbon dots coated Sn nanocompositew with blue emission could be obtained through direct ultrasonic treatment. As compared with the previous reports, the FCNs obtained from this work using the cigarette ash and PEG-SH emits green-yellow color. It indicates that the cigarette ash with aromatic rings has been modified with PEG-SH. The relative large conjugation systems will result in long emission wavelength under similar excitation wavelength.

The remarkable fluorescence stability of fluorescent nanomaterials under extreme conditions is an important parameter for their potential biological applications. Therefore, we further measured the detailed fluorescence property of FCNs under long-term irradiation and at different pH values. The change of the fluorescence intensity with different pH values is shown in Fig. 7 A. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity maintained a modest level under the weak acid and strong base conditions. Furthermore, we find that the fluorescence of FCNs is more stable under the basis environment than under the acidic environment. Fig. 7B shows the relationship of the fluorescence strength versus he irradiating time of an aqueous solution of FCNs. We illuminated the aqueous solution of FCNs with a 365 nm UV lamp for 2 h and measured its fluorescence intensity at different timepoints. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the aqueous solution of FCNs decreased from 488 a.u. to 450 a.u. and only lost 7.8% fluorescence intensity after irradiation for 2 h. The above results indicate remarkable the fluorescence stability of FCNs under extreme conditions, which makes them promising for potential biological applications.

PEG is a commercially available hydrophilic polymer with excellent biocompatibility. PEG has been widely employed in biomedical applications over the past several decades. In this work, PEG not only improves the water dispersibility of FCNs but also promotes their biocompatibility. Thus, we further evaluated the cell cytotoxicity of FCNs using L929 cells through the CKK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 8 , the L929 cells were cultured and maintained with 20–160 μg mL−1 FCNs for 8 and 24 h. No obvious cell viability decrease was observed, and the percentage of survival cells remained at more than 91% with 160 µg mL−1 FCNs. Therefore, the obtained FCNs showed no apparent toxicity to the cells. The good biocompatibility implies the good potential of FCNs for biomedical applications.

Considering their bright green fluorescence, resistance to photobleaching, great water dispersibility and low cytotoxicity, these PEG-functionalized FCNs are promising candidates for biomedical applications. Therefore, the cell uptake behavior of these PEG functionalized FCNs was evaluated via CLSM observation. The cells were labeled with the synthetic FCNs after they were incubated in a medium containing 50 μg mL−1 FCNs for 3 h. As displayed in Fig. 9 A, the FCNs were able to light all cells. The fluorescence signals were from a formation of spindle morphology with a size of approximately several micrometers. It should be noted that only weak or no fluorescence was observed at the center positions. These center areas are likely the locations of the cell nuclei. Thus, we speculate that these PEG-functionalized FCNs are easily taken up by cells through endocytosis and are mainly located at the cytoplasm. In addition, Fig. 9B shows bright field images of cells. All cells still strongly adhered to the cell plates and kept their normal morphology. This further implied the low toxicity and good biocompatibility of PEG-functionalized FCNs. Some aggregates with strong contrast were also found at the cell locations. These aggregates are attributed to the aggregation of FCNs. More importantly, we found that the fluorescence signals only covered the cell locations, which is further evidence that PEG-functionalized FCNs are internalized by cells. Based on the CLSM images, we conclude that these PEG functionalized FCNs can be effectively taken up by cells through nonspecific endocytosis. These results imply that these PEG functionalized FCNs can likely be used as fluorescent probes for biological imaging and other related biomedical applications.

In conclusion, we developed a green and inexpensive method to synthesize high-quality PEG-functionalized FCNs using cigarette ash and thiol-group-terminated PEG as precursors through a one-pot ultrasonic irradiation treatment. Multitudinous characterization techniques demonstrated their uniform nanoscale size, remarkable optical stability under long-term UV light irradiation and various pH values, excellent water dispersibility and great biocompatibility. Combined with their effective cell uptake performance as well as their strong fluorescence intensity, these PEG = functionalized FCNs should have great potential for biological imaging and other biomedical applications. Furthermore, compared with common preparation strategies, this method based on a one-pot ultrasonic irradiation treatment only requires simple experimental procedures and benign reaction conditions (e.g., a low reaction temperature, air atmosphere and short reaction time) [51]. Moreover, we opted to use cigarette ash as the carbon source, which is a cheap and renewable biomaterial. More importantly, some other thiol-group-terminating molecules could also be introduced on the surface of FCNs through the facile one-pot ultrasonic irradiation treatment. Furthermore, other FCNs will also be fabricated using other materials as the precusors. Therefore, we believe this work will be of great interest for the development of FCNs with designable properties and functions. The further development of these FCNs for other biomedical applications such as drug delivery and fabrication of multifunctional theranostic systems will be reported in our future studies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014, 21788102), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066).
